zh:
玛雅神话生长在尤卡坦半岛的灰岩平原与危地马拉的火山高地之间。它的文献命运奇特：古典期（公元250—900年）的石碑、彩绘陶器与神庙铭文留下了最早的神谱，四部幸存的树皮纸手抄本（德累斯顿、马德里、巴黎、格罗利尔）保存着祭司的历书与诸神图像，而叙事最完整的一部书却诞生于征服之后——16世纪中叶，基切贵族用拉丁字母写定《波波尔·乌》，把创世、双子英雄与王朝谱系一并存进纸页。兰达主教焚毁玛雅书籍的同时，他的《尤卡坦纪事》又阴差阳错地成了解读这一切的钥匙。

这套神话的骨架是玉米与循环。诸神造人三试三改，泥人溃于水、木人亡于洪，直到白黄玉米磨成的面团捏出真正的人类——人是玉米做的，种植与收割因此成了一遍遍重演的神话。大地之下是名为“恐惧之地”的西巴尔巴，洞穴与灰岩坑就是它的门口；死神以球赛与凶屋收割来客，而乌纳普与斯巴兰克两兄弟用萤火虫、假头颅和一场献祭戏法赢了回来。天上的雨神恰克挥斧劈出闪电，老年造物主伊察姆纳坐在天带宝座上执掌文字与历法，羽蛇库库尔坎每年春分与秋分沿着奇琴伊察的金字塔阶梯爬下来。

与地中海诸神的恩怨剧不同，玛雅神话的趣味在于机智：胜负多靠圈套、易容与将计就计，连死神都栽在自己求看的戏法上。它也从未真正落幕——数百万玛雅语使用者至今生活在原地，旱年的求雨仪式照旧在田间搭起祭台，高地的日守者仍按二百六十日的历法数着日子。
en:
Maya mythology grew up between the limestone plains of the Yucatán Peninsula and the volcanic highlands of Guatemala. Its written record has a strange shape: the earliest portraits of the gods survive on Classic period (250-900 CE) stelae, painted vases, and temple inscriptions; four bark-paper codices (Dresden, Madrid, Paris, Grolier) preserve priestly almanacs and divine imagery; yet the fullest narrative was set down only after the conquest, when K'iche' nobles in the mid-16th century used the Latin alphabet to record the Popol Vuh - creation, Hero Twins, and dynastic genealogy in one book. Bishop Landa burned the Maya libraries, and his own Relación then became, by accident, a key to reading what remained.

The skeleton of this mythology is maize and recurrence. The gods made humanity three times over: people of mud dissolved, people of wood perished under the flood and a rain of burning resin, and only dough ground from white and yellow corn yielded true human beings. People are made of maize, so planting and harvest replay the myth every year. Beneath the earth lies Xibalba, the "place of fright," entered through caves and cenotes, where death lords harvest their guests with ballgames and killing houses - until the twins Hunahpu and Xbalanque beat them with fireflies, a substitute head, and one irresistible sacrifice trick. Above, the rain god Chaac strikes lightning from his axe, the aged creator Itzamna rules writing and the calendar from his sky-band throne, and the feathered serpent Kukulkan crawls down the pyramid stairway at Chichén Itzá once every equinox.

Where Mediterranean gods feud, Maya gods are out-thought: victories turn on traps, disguises, and schemes turned back on the schemer, and even the lords of death fall for a trick they begged to watch. Nor did any of it truly end - millions of Maya speakers still live on the same land, rain ceremonies still raise altars in the fields in dry years, and highland daykeepers still count the 260-day round.
